name: Moroccan Magic
description: Moroccan magic, deeply rooted in Berber, Arab, and African traditions, encompasses a diverse range of practices, from protective amulets and healing rituals to divination and love spells. It draws upon Islamic mysticism, pre-Islamic beliefs, and local folklore, often involving the invocation of spirits (jinn), saints (awliya), and the use of Quranic verses. The practice is often secretive, passed down through families or from master to apprentice. It plays a significant role in addressing everyday concerns, such as health, relationships, and financial well-being, offering solace and solutions outside the realm of conventional medicine or societal structures. The use of herbs, oils, incense, and specific prayers are common elements. While some aspects are considered permissible within Islamic beliefs, others are viewed with suspicion or outright forbidden, leading to a complex relationship between magic and religion in Moroccan society. The power of belief and intention are central, shaping the effectiveness of any magical act. Its origins are ancient, blending indigenous beliefs with influences from various cultures that have shaped Morocco's history. This creates a resilient and dynamic system that continues to evolve.
subtitle: A Tapestry of Spirits and Spells

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Sidi Abd er-Rahman al-Majdub
keyMyths.description: Sidi Abd er-Rahman al-Majdub was a wandering Sufi mystic known for his prophetic verses and connection to the spiritual realm. His words are often invoked for guidance and blessings in various magical practices.

keyMyths.title: The Power of the Jinn
keyMyths.description: Jinn are supernatural beings that can be both benevolent and malevolent, playing a significant role in Moroccan magic. They can be summoned for assistance or protection, but must be treated with respect and caution.

sources.author: Edward Westermarck
sources.description: A classic anthropological study of Moroccan customs and beliefs, including magical practices.
sources.text: A classic anthropological study of Moroccan customs and beliefs, including magical practices.

sources.author: Vincent Crapanzano
sources.work: The Hamadsha: A Study in Moroccan Ethnopsychiatry
sources.description: Explores the intersection of religion, healing, and possession in the Hamadsha Sufi order.
sources.text: Explores the intersection of religion, healing, and possession in the Hamadsha Sufi order.
